narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 51.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision against accepting your application. This prediction is based on a close look at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details compared to thousands of past students. You ranked in the lowest decile for your school's Year 1 grades, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position, and the same held true for your Year 3 performance. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 is noticeably lower than the average of 3.21, and your LSAT score of 36 falls just short of the usual 36.7. Coming from a lower-middle income family also played a role, unlike many applicants from higher brackets.

Among all these factors, your Year 1 decile ranking had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, followed closely by your Year 3 decile, then family income, LSAT, undergraduate GPA, racial background as White, gender as male, and full-time enrollment status. Each of these nudged the model's thinking slightly more toward expecting a bar exam struggle rather than success, with your early low rankings carrying the most weight in this borderline case. Compared to typical students—who often have stronger early grades, higher GPAs, and better LSATs—your profile stood out as riskier for bar passage, even though demographics like being a white male full-timer align with the majority. It's a tough call, but the model prioritizes signals of academic readiness that have historically predicted first-time bar success.

Looking ahead, you can realistically boost your odds by focusing on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above the average, build stronger grades in any ongoing studies to lift those decile rankings, and seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for students from similar income backgrounds. Programs offering LSAT tutoring or GPA-boosting post-bac courses have helped others in your shoes turn things around. With dedication, these steps could shift future predictions in your favor and open doors to law school. Hang in there—you've got the drive to rewrite this story.
